Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time in the evenings after work, but at 29 with a lot going on, things rarely went according to plan. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often just listen to lecture recordings while doing other things around the house. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until panic set in.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what else was happening in my life. I have extracurriculars and responsibilities that don't pause just because I have coursework. Deadlines definitely drove when I'd actually sit down and focus. Regular times? That sounds nice in theory, but my reality was more like cramming when absolutely necessary.
Q3: Usually it was either guilt or a deadline notification that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email reminder about something due and that would push me to check what I'd missed. Other times I'd log in just to see how far behind I was, which honestly just made me feel worse. It wasn't exactly a motivating experience most of the time.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine—it always felt like a chore I was avoiding. There were maybe a couple of weeks mid-semester where I checked in more regularly, but that faded quickly. I'd forget about it until something urgent popped up again.
Q5: The recorded lectures were genuinely helpful for me since I learn best by listening. I'd play them while commuting or doing housework. The discussion boards were occasionally useful when other students explained things in simpler terms than the professor did. But most other resources just sat there untouched.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the readings and supplementary materials. They felt overwhelming, and I kept telling myself I'd get to them later. The written resources just didn't stick for me the way audio content did. I also barely touched the optional practice quizzes—they felt like extra work when I was already struggling to keep up with the basics.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see an immediate connection to passing the course. If it was going to be on an exam or counted toward my grade, I'd make myself do it. Anything that felt abstract or like busywork got pushed to the bottom of my list and usually forgotten entirely.
Q8: Optional activities felt like suggestions I could safely ignore, and I usually did. Required ones carried this weight of obligation that I couldn't escape, even if I procrastinated on them. There's something about knowing something is optional that made it easy for my brain to dismiss it completely, even when I knew it might help me.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lecture recordings and listened to them repeatedly offline. I also borrowed a textbook from a friend and studied from that quite a bit. Some of my best studying happened with just my notes and a cup of coffee, completely disconnected from the internet. The LMS wouldn't know any of that happened.
Q10: There were whole weekends where I'd review my handwritten notes and listen to downloaded lectures on repeat, but I never logged into the system once. I'd also talk through concepts with a coworker who took similar courses, which really helped me understand things. None of that shows up anywhere in the data.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download a lecture or PDF once and then use it for weeks without going back to the LMS. It was easier to have things saved locally where I could access them without dealing with slow loading times or needing internet. The LMS probably thought I only looked at something once when I'd actually reviewed it multiple times.
Q12: There were definitely periods where everything felt impossible. Around midterms, my stress levels were through the roof, and I felt genuinely scared about failing. Work got intense at the same time, and I just couldn't bring myself to engage with course materials. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped a little, but the fear didn't really go away.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by everything I had to do, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. Confusion about assignments made me avoid them rather than seek help—I'd just freeze up. My competing responsibilities always seemed more urgent than coursework, even when logically I knew the course mattered.
Q14: Oh, plenty of times. I'd open a video and let it play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without actually absorbing anything anyone said. My eyes were on the screen, but my brain had checked out. It was like going through the motions without any real learning happening.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The fear of doing them badly made me avoid starting them at all. It's like this paralysis where the anxiety about the task becomes bigger than the task itself. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same excuse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me use the LMS consistently. I'd ignore everything until a deadline loomed, then frantically try to catch up. It wasn't healthy, and my assignment completion rate shows that—I missed more than I'd like to admit. The deadline pressure was stressful but also the only thing that got me moving.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Good feedback gave me a brief boost, but it faded quickly. Critical feedback made me want to avoid the course entirely for a while. I know that's not a productive response, but when you're already feeling scared and stressed, negative feedback just reinforces that you're not good enough.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but having ten online courses to juggle was overwhelming. I couldn't give proper attention to any single one. The structure of this particular course didn't account for students like me who are balancing a lot. Weekly modules felt relentless when I was already behind.
Q19: The flexibility of accessing materials anytime was helpful in theory, but it also meant I could always push things off. Clearer deadlines and maybe some check-ins would have forced me to stay on track. The discussion requirements were manageable, but the sheer volume of content made it hard to know what to prioritize.
Q20: I think that happens when someone is trying to figure things out but can't translate that into completed work. Maybe they're confused, overwhelmed, or dealing with personal issues. High activity might mean they're searching for understanding but hitting walls. It doesn't mean they're not trying—sometimes trying looks messy.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. I passed with decent exam scores despite low LMS activity because I studied a lot offline. Listening to lectures repeatedly and reviewing notes on my own time worked better than clicking through online modules. The system doesn't capture that kind of learning at all.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually learned anything. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They're measuring activity, not understanding or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials—it's about being mentally present. For me, engagement has been a struggle this semester because fear and stress kept pulling me away from that focused state.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I've been throughout this course, or how much that fear affected my ability to engage. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to lectures while anxious, or the assignments I avoided because I was terrified of failing. My emotional state shaped everything about how I participated, and none of that is captured in the data.
